speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five, the interview with Lillian Bielsky-Bell, December 1st, 1996. You said that one partisan was not killed because he hid under the stove. And at night he came out from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The chicken coop.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was underneath the chicken coop.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, when the family was discussing this, so proud of themselves that they did such a marvelous thing. They helped the Germans. They killed another ten Jews. He came out. He said, this won't be forgotten. It will come the day that you'll be smashed the same way. Then some of his sons came with a hack-- a sword-- get him over the head. Took him out right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer's son got up and killed the last boy that was hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how do you know this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I know this story? When the-- they didn't show up for a while, more than the time that was assigned for them, intelligence from our brigade was looking for them. And you know, there is no secret. So somebody probably put them in that direction. And then my husband sent out his brother, which was in charge, always, of the fighting group, [? assail, ?] and he put him-- he told them not a thing should be touched in this house. Not a thing, no matter how tempting it is, because usually they took a blanket, they took a scarf, they took a warm coat, whatever they found to take.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, not a thing. Everything should go with fire with them together. I should be put in-- put out a sign. That's what's happening to people that are trying to kill-- keep clothes for the Germans, trying to-- to help them, and get rid of Jews. And that will happen to anybody else who will do like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one guy-- I just saw him a week ago-- could not resist. He saw a beautiful jacket, and he took it. He asked. He asked the commander that was with them if I can take it. He said, go ahead and take it. And in the jacket, in the back of his jacket, in the inside pocket, he found a letter-- a German letter-- which was placed in Ro-- in Minsk when they started, you know, after the war. The SA document said-- big letter from the Germans-- thank you so much for helping us out to-- to kill the Jews, to find them, and for sending your son to-- to the place-- to the right place. And you'll be awarded for it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They award anybody 400,000 ruble for-- to catch my husband, to-- to catch him or to kill him or to bring him alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the-- how was the work divided? Once the group became a sizable group, there were many people in that group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It started slowly-- 300, 400, 750. Then we had to go to a very big-- the Germans made a big-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Raid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raid on us. And we were ten days in-- in water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody survived-- small kids. This was already in [INAUDIBLE] This was already in the deep woods of the labor camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In dumps and water. No food, no nothing. And every-- not one person was missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was-- how was the work divided when the group was so big?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- when the group was already so big, it was established-- my husband had a choice-- I told you. To stay with the older and young, and children, or to leave them and-- and go on his own. He never accepted it, no matter how. And believe me, there was plenty problems. As if-- as today, there's different problems in life. It was problems because our Jews cannot sit still. Like I mentioned, they wanted-- everybody wanted to be in the command. Commander can be the only one. And they find different things. They found different things to tell and to go to the Russian-- there was a-- my husband wasn't the commander of the partisans. He was a commander of his brigade, but he has the head bosses over him that he had to listen to and obey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the bosses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sent from-- from Russia that they had their main headquarters. And the planes from Russia used to come there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of the-- was that the Russian Army, or was it the-- the Polish partisans connected with the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. That was the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Directly with the Russian Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a particular commander of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Platon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Platon. What-- do you know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the whole boss of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a General?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And our Jews weren't lazy to go and complain, and say that he's not a communist, that he's not having the communist party the right way At that time, in order-- you had to be a-- you know, to-- they should like you. They should-- they should be good friends with you. And they tried from all sides. There were plenty of complaints from the inside people to the main management.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Tuvia stayed in the family camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuvia stayed. We-- we went out, 1,250 people. He wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. You couldn't behave with the family camp without fighters. They took-- the Russians tried to, not once, to take away from us, to divide us, to take away some fighters from us. But he built it again. He did it again. And nobody was willing-- very little were willing to leave the camp. They wanted to stay with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the fighters live together with the family people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all one camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: One camp. And workshops, one camp, a bakery, a salami factory, a soap factory, a-- no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They used to-- no, like a bath, but they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: A steam bath.

speaker: interviewee
text: A steam bath. They used to take turns, you know. There was plenty of flies, plenty of in-- of insects. It's woods. And each one had his own turn, and when the time came, he had to go and take care in it. There were doctors. There was a hospital. There were shoemakers. There were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every little thing what a normal life needs, there were there. And the-- more the sen-- the seniority was more occupied with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the older people were in charge of the different workshops.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who decided who would do which work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever Tuvia-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some were volunteers that they know how to do this, a watchmaker or this, or even for the rifles, for the guns. You needed-- you had to clean, you had something that breaks, something-- everything was organized. People from before the war knew some work, and that's where they volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing? What was your skill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I was-- at that time, I was already with Tuvia. So a lot of-- from other-- from other brigades came always visiting. We had a kindergarten. We had shows. We had-- we had-- we had to receive them. We had to, you know, to host them. And I was staying on guard, too. And when I'd see my husband in the mornings, he said, Mr. So-and-So, Mr. Commander, let me tell you that the night was quiet. Nobody showed up. Nobody-- no shooting, no this, no that. I wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. I never played the role of a Miss Commander-- Mrs. Commander. Never. Whoever came from outside, I tried always to host them, to give them, to help them. But you know, you can not be [INAUDIBLE]. They'll find anyway something to say on you. And once you're in commend, there is always something to say because you cannot satisfy the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how the children were taken care of. Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. We had children that they wanted-- that-- the parents didn't want to send them. But they were-- they wanted-- the Russian people wanted to have them in Russia, and to go on, you know, with their lives, because they were very talented in singing and dancing. We had a kindergarten with a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was the teacher? [? LB: ?] [? Sechel. ?] She was-- she was from the Polish region that came in '39 and escaped. And she came to Lida, and then she came to the woods, and-- How-- did the children obey the discipline as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. In fact, when we were raided, and children-- they were sitting in water without food. They just said that's, I think, from God, because not a boo, not a cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many children under the age of 12, let's say, 12, 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had probably about 10 children like this. Children were born in the woods too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- when-- when a woman was in labor and childbirth, who helped them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We helped. We had quite a few doctors. In fact, other brigades used to come to our doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the relationships between men and women? Were there-- you said before that there were marriages.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were marriages before the war, and there were marriages, you know, that [INAUDIBLE] or a woman-- or a wife was killed or a husband was killed, or when children were killed. The main thing were the children, because they didn't leave too many children alive, only the ones that escaped. That's was their aim-- the German's aim, to kill the children. That was their pleasure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some were very lonely. They lost everybody. They were just alone. And the circumstances weren't too good. Individually, Tuvia couldn't take care on everybody, individually. So if you had a man around you, he was worried about you-- to bring you the food, to bring you a pair of socks, or a pair of-- of shoes or whatever he saw that he could grab from a pa-- from a farm or from a village. You were more secured having somebody at the site. But not everybody could do this again too. Some of them had a few. Some of them didn't have nobody. That's-- that was their own decision. Some of them didn't want to, they-- as they came, so they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were women in the fighting group also?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried. Not too many, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go with Tuvia to, let's say, when he had to go places, but-- you know, to meet somebody or some-- some special place. There were Russian holidays, special holidays, they used to observe, and we had to observe too. We had shows that the Russian came to us. You had to host them. The kids performed. But-- but to say that I was a fighter? No. I wasn't a fighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn how to shoot a gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried it a few times, but I tried not to do that. Just my teaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you ask them today, they'll sure did. But I don't-- I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there wasn't. That's why I cannot remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What were the jobs that the women did, as opposed to the jobs that the men did?

speaker: interviewee
text: This and they-- they-- some of them worked in the kitchen. Some of them worked in the bakery. Some of them worked in the-- in the-- where they had the-- it was a long-- a long, special building, made for-- for the shoemakers, for the dressmakers, for the-- for the barbers, for everybody. It was like a, you know, a flea market, that everybody had a station where he worked. A lot of people worked, and a lot of people did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe those buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm telling you. It just came to my mind. It looked like a flea market, booths by booths by booths, and there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they build it? How did they construct it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like they do barracks. It was a big barrack. A long--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a bo-- you said earlier that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when we leaved, but this was on top, because we were already in the deeper woods. So this was on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the living quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living quarters were there too, on top, in the-- in the deep woods in the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they made of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends from who-- who built what. That's-- the people themselves had to build. Whatever the-- some did it from wood. Some did it from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- you know, they had to be covered and look like the trees on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camouflage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camouflage. Or in the beginning, when we started, if you walked out and there was snow, you had the strainer and you covered with the snow-- the-- the snow bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean you covered over your footsteps.

speaker: interviewee
text: The steps. Footsteps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about where you lived with Tuvia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was that structure like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing fancy. Believe me, nothing fancy. It was structured from wood, from the wood that they had in the woods. They couldn't structure from anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: From logs?

speaker: interviewee
text: From logs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who built it?

speaker: interviewee
text: For us, Tuvia didn't build it. There were people that built it. But each one, that if he wanted nicer quarters or nicer place to live, they used to build for themselves. And the beds they built too, from the same wood logs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- what about water? What was your source of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well that's the first that we used to find-- is water wells. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they dig a well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dig. Dig. Dig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about medical care?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medical care-- many people especially went-- like I mentioned [? Kozlowski, ?] in the beginning, used to bring. But later on, the people that came from the ghettos and from all over. It wasn't a supply from all kind of antibiotics, that's for sure. In very serious cases, we used-- we used sulfa at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sulfa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that came from the ghettos. The fresh ones that came from the ghettos, or the doctors that came to our-- to us. They had it supplies. They stealed from the Germans and they brought along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people had pills, too, that they said, if they'll fall alive in the hands of the Germans, they want to kill themselves. They want to take a pill and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean poison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody ever do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It wasn't so bad in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened-- when people went out on raids and they got wounded, who took care of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a hospital with nurses and doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that located in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, aside. In case there's a raid on the camp, there should be aside.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get news from the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, Tuvia had a radio, which was given to him from one of his, you know, intelligence-- from the-- not Jewish intelligence, but from his friends from once upon a time, that he had them. Second of all, they-- they used== the farmers used to go to the-- to the cities [INAUDIBLE] bring newspaper. Not every day, but more or less, we knew what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody keep a record of the life there in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean if-- we had some very intellectuals. We had a professor that kept a diary. But then he was arrested and everything was taken from him, by crossing the border of Poland to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Amarat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His wife is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Jewish religious life? Was there anything of that in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. We knew every holiday. And there was a special group from older people that observed, who had the shochet. You know what a shochet means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll explain to you. How do you say a shochet in--

speaker: interviewer
text: A slaughterer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slaughterer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He had- he-- in fact, he has a few daughters, and when he used to come with Tuvia, all five daughters were waiting for us at the airport. Because they came-- five daughters and a husband-- a father and a mother-- to the woods. So there were seven people survived form [? Veranova. ?] And he was like the-- the rabbi of all the religious people. So one time-- I mentioned before Platon-- he was the boss of all the partisans, and he ruled, the General Platon. And he came-- he decided one time, he had so much complaints about that they were eating, were not doing nothing, that Tuvia does this, that [? Tuvia does ?] that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he called Tuvia one time and another time, and he tried to explain to him that it's not so. So he said, you know what-- he said to him, you know what? You come to the base and take a look for yourself. And-- and then you maybe all your answer-- questions will be answered. And he did. It took a while, but he did. Oh, we received him beautifully. He went-- he couldn't get over it. He couldn't get over the kids. He couldn't get over the-- the workshops. He couldn't get over nothing. [INAUDIBLE] in camp where leather was in a big pail. What did they-- what did they use the leather for? Repairing shoes. So they used to put the leather in water, and I don't know how they did it. But it was-- at the-- and they-- they had a lot of use of the leather, what they-- sometimes they used to make a pair of boots, a pair of shoes. And this was a very big barrack for the leather. It was pretty smelly because leather smells.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there, they used to come, for [NON-ENGLISH], for the holidays. And the shochet was on top of them all. And sure enough, Platon walked in there and looked and wondered how-- how are they making the leather, how are this. He couldn't-- he couldn't believe his eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what are you doing. Those people that are saying and-- and all the time [NON-ENGLISH]? So Tuvia was afraid to tell him that they are davening. So he said to him, you know, they're all learning the-- the Bible of the communism and the-- the Marxism and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he couldn't-- you know, I can't believe it. Yeah, he says. We have all kind of groups, that each one is learning something else. And so today, they-- all the people have nothing to do so they are learning the story of the communists. And he believed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this shochet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. But was a time, I-- I-- it's very-- if it's important, I'll find out, it's-- because I have somebody to find out. But he-- there were seven people there. His five daughters are still in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the holidays celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew when it's Pesach. We could-- we knew-- we baked matzohs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE] But they-- this group from the-- this-- they baked matzohs. They marked it, the holidays. Listen, we couldn't do these things because we didn't know when we had to run. It wasn't that I'm sitting in the house and if it's raining I don't-- there was nowhere I-- they didn't-- no rain, and no snow, and no wind, and no nothing. You want to survive, so you go against everything. You run, you go, you-- you fall, you get up, you go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever lose hope that you would manage, that you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. Me? Yeah. I'm a, by nature, a pessimist. That's what they say, or-- I don't feel like this, but that's what they all. But Tuvia always was optimistic. Not everybody, but some of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the-- were there different groups within the large group? Were there-- like the people from [? Navahrudak, ?] did-- did they form their own little group within the large group? Or were there good relationships?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cliques?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Were there cliques?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were cliques.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: One city didn't bother with the other. You know, everybody-- you must understand now that everybody was afraid, so much. They wanted so much to survive, that they thought, if another person-- there were plenty of people that were against it. We came, the first ones to the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were saying, what will we do with food? How will we feed them? That time, it wasn't organized. When they chose my husband as a commander, it was already the seven people that I mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they went into a meeting and they chose him as a commander because he had military knowledge, he served in the Polish Army, he was good in languages, he was good in speech, and he was good in organizing. And he was the oldest. So they-- they choosed him. But-- so each one that came from the ghetto, they were afraid. Maybe he will take away a piece from me. Maybe he will take away a piece from me. It was-- they didn't click together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they didn't trust each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say they didn't trust. They were afraid that-- that they'll take away from him something. Maybe it will be my-- maybe we'll get less soup by-- by some other people coming than-- than he used to, you know? It was a-- it was a kitchen for everybody. It was a kitchen for the-- for the special milk for the children, more.

speaker: interviewee
text: But for the elderly, he tried to pay more attention, you know, like, to give them a bigger supply. But they were afraid that each one-- that they'll take away from me something. Maybe it won't be enough for me. And people are funny. They're-- if you want to live, why shouldn't I now want to live? So how did Tuvia cope with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very hard. With other Jewish people, it's not easy to go because we have our ideas. We have our mindset. And very-- it's very hard sometimes to-- to cope with them. Not-- very-- they gave him a lot of trouble. Somebody else in his place, not with his strong nature and with his disciplined hand, we'd never survive with so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if somebody disobeyed the discipline-- didn't follow orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had a-- was sent to jail. We had a jail, too. Two people were killed for disobeying intentionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a trial, actually. It was a trial that was a decision. WE had somebody like a prosecutor always, that he finished law in-- in Europe. And he was the one to make the decisions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who decided that those two people-- what-- what was their offense? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted to make a group into-- in the same group to go against Tuvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: To break up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who decided that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that worked on it. He was in jail. He didn't quiet down. He did his-- what he felt to do. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't go with it. Tuvia was-- had nothing to do with it. There were people that felt the-- their-- whatever was coming to them. Listen. One is not 1,250. To destroy this much was-- was unnecessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here.
